title: Catching and handling chickens
subtitle: The low-stress approach to picking up and examining a hen.
excerpt: Catching a hen without a chase is possible once you understand how chickens move and where they feel safe. This covers the catch-at-night method, the daytime corner approach, correct carrying positions, and what a basic hand examination should cover when you pick up any bird.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Welfare Code of Practice for Laying Hens (2018), handling guidance. Smallholder Magazine, low-stress poultry handling techniques.

glossaryTerms.definition: The external opening of the cloaca, through which both eggs and droppings pass. A healthy vent is clean, pink, and moist; a soiled, prolapsed, or pale vent indicates a problem.
The breastbone running down the centre of the chest. A sharp keel with no muscle either side indicates a bird that is underweight and either ill or not getting enough feed.

content.text: A hen that is chased across the run before being caught is stressed for 20 to 30 minutes afterward. Catching at night, when birds are settled on the perch and calm, is the standard approach for routine handling, wing clipping, vet inspection, and moving birds. The daytime corner method is an alternative for birds that are not tame. The goal in both cases is one smooth confident movement rather than a hesitant grab.

Enter the coop after dark using a red-tinted torch (red light does not disturb birds the way white light does). Approach a perching bird from the side and place both hands firmly and quickly around the body, wings against the sides, before the bird can flap. Grip with confidence: a hesitant, light grip causes more struggling than a firm one.

Herd the bird gently into a corner using your arms outstretched, then crouch and approach slowly. Do not make sudden movements. Once the bird is in the corner and not moving, reach forward and place one hand over the back before it can duck sideways, then gather the body with both hands. In a larger group, moving a single bird into a small pen or box before catching reduces the chance of panicking the whole flock.

The standard carrying position tucks the bird under one arm, with the breast against your body, the tail pointing behind you, and both feet held together in the same hand with the fingers between the legs to prevent grip cuts. The head looks forward, away from your body. This position immobilises the wings without restricting breathing and leaves one hand free.
Any time you pick up a hen, run a 60-second check: feel the
bone for muscle coverage (a sharp keel with no muscle is a bird that is losing condition), check the

for fouling or signs of prolapse, feel the crop for impaction or emptiness late in the day, and look at the eyes and nostrils for discharge. A hen that feels normal on all four counts is almost certainly healthy; a hen that fails one of them warrants a closer look before she goes back in with the flock.
